choice_c: DARPANET was the first cellular telephone network developed by the US military in 1975
choice_d: DARPANET was a competing protocol suite to TCP/IP that was eventually replaced in the early 2000s
explanation: Historical significance: ARPANET 1969 nodes — UCLA; SRI; UC Santa Barbara; U of Utah. First packet-switched WAN. NCP (Network Control Protocol) replaced by TCP/IP on Jan 1 1983 (Flag Day). TCP/IP adoption enabled interconnection of disparate networks. ARPANET decommissioned 1990 — its successor became the global internet. Core principle: design for resilience — packet switching allows multiple paths; no single point of failure. This design philosophy persists in modern internet routing.
aspect_key: independent packet forwarding and possible out-of-order arrival

question: What is a TCP SYN flood DDoS attack and what design feature of TCP makes it effective?
choice_a: A SYN flood floods the target with valid completed connections that consume all server threads
choice_b: A SYN flood exploits TCP's half-open connection state — the attacker sends thousands of SYN packets with spoofed source IPs; for each SYN the server allocates a connection entry; sends SYN-ACK; and waits for the third ACK; the spoofed source IPs never respond; the server's connection table fills with half-open entries each waiting for the ACK timeout (typically 75 seconds); when the table is full legitimate clients' SYN packets are dropped — the server appears down; the design feature exploited is that TCP allocates state (memory) based on the SYN before the connection is confirmed
choice_c: A SYN flood attack only works on UDP — TCP's three-way handshake prevents this attack
choice_d: SYN floods exhaust the target's bandwidth — the attack is a volumetric attack not a state exhaustion attack
explanation: SYN flood mitigation: SYN cookies — server encodes connection state in the initial sequence number instead of allocating a table entry; memory is only allocated when the legitimate ACK arrives with the correct sequence number. Firewall rate limiting: limit SYN rate per source IP. TCP syncache: separate smaller structure for half-open connections. Modern DDoS protection: scrubbing centers verify TCP handshakes before forwarding to the server.
aspect_key: reliable sequencing; ack-driven retransmission; and handshake control

question: How does CIDR route aggregation (supernetting) reduce the size of internet routing tables?
choice_a: CIDR aggregation increases routing table size because it adds entries for every subnet in a block
choice_b: CIDR allows multiple contiguous address blocks to be represented by a single routing table entry — for example an ISP that has been allocated 203.0.113.0/24 through 203.0.116.0/24 (four /24 blocks) can advertise a single 203.0.112.0/22 route to its upstream providers instead of four separate /24 entries; this reduces routing table entries; before CIDR classful routing required separate entries for every Class A; B; and C network causing exponential table growth; CIDR's aggregation capability was essential to prevent the global routing table from becoming unmanageable
choice_c: CIDR aggregation is only applied within a single organization's network; internet routing still uses classful routes
choice_d: Route aggregation requires all four subnets to be the same size — mixed /24 and /25 blocks cannot be aggregated
explanation: Aggregation math: 203.0.112.0/24; 203.0.113.0/24; 203.0.114.0/24; 203.0.115.0/24 share the same first 22 bits → summarize as 203.0.112.0/22. BGP advertisement: one /22 instead of four /24 routes. Internet routing table (2024): ~1 million routes. Without CIDR: classful routing would have required entries for every assigned Class C block — potentially tens of millions of entries. CIDR reduced this by enabling hierarchical aggregation.
aspect_key: classless prefix-length notation enables flexible subnet sizing

question: A host at 192.168.50.200 with mask 255.255.255.128 attempts to communicate with 192.168.50.130. Show the complete binary AND to determine if these hosts are on the same subnet and state what forwarding decision the host makes.
choice_a: Both addresses are always in the same /24 subnet so the mask doesn't matter for this communication
choice_b: Mask 255.255.255.128 last octet = 10000000. Host 200 = 11001000 AND 10000000 = 10000000 = 128. Host 130 = 10000010 AND 10000000 = 10000000 = 128. Both AND results = 128 → both are in the 192.168.50.128/25 subnet → same subnet → the source host sends an ARP request for 192.168.50.130 and communicates directly without using the default gateway
choice_c: 200 AND 128 = 128 and 130 AND 128 = 0 so they are in different subnets
choice_d: The mask 255.255.255.128 is invalid — subnet masks must be multiples of 8 bits
explanation: /25 subnet analysis: 192.168.50.128/25 range = .128 to .255. Both .200 and .130 fall in this range. Binary proof: 200=1100 1000; mask=1000 0000; AND=1000 0000=128. 130=1000 0010; mask=1000 0000; AND=1000 0000=128. Same network address (128) → same subnet. ARP resolution: host sends ARP broadcast; .130 replies; communication proceeds directly.
aspect_key: octet conversion between binary and decimal for subnet calculations

question: A network engineer is performing VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking) on 10.10.0.0/16. Department A needs 500 hosts; Department B needs 100 hosts; Department C needs 50 hosts; and Department D needs 10 hosts. Assign the most efficient subnet to each department showing the mask and starting address in allocation order.
choice_a: All departments should receive /24 subnets regardless of size to simplify administration
choice_b: Allocate largest-first to avoid wasted space: Dept A (500 hosts needs 9 bits = /23; 2^9-2=510): 10.10.0.0/23. Dept B (100 hosts needs 7 bits = /25; 2^7-2=126): 10.10.2.0/25. Dept C (50 hosts needs 6 bits = /26; 2^6-2=62): 10.10.2.128/26. Dept D (10 hosts needs 4 bits = /28; 2^4-2=14): 10.10.2.192/28. Each allocation starts immediately after the previous subnet boundary ensuring efficient use of the 10.10.0.0/16 space
choice_c: VLSM requires all subnets to be the same prefix length — each department must use the same mask
choice_d: Variable masks cannot be used in the same address space — each department needs a separate /16 block
aspect_key: subnet mask and prefix split determine network id vs host space

question: An attacker on the local network sends a gratuitous ARP advertising that the default gateway's IP (192.168.1.1) belongs to the attacker's MAC address. What happens to all other hosts on the segment; what attack is this; and what is the switch-level mitigation?
choice_a: Gratuitous ARPs are always ignored by hosts — hosts only update ARP cache from replies to their own requests
choice_b: All hosts that receive the gratuitous ARP update their ARP cache to map 192.168.1.1 to the attacker's MAC — all subsequent traffic destined for outside the local subnet is sent to the attacker's MAC instead of the real router; the attacker becomes an on-path (MITM) intermediary and can read; modify; or drop traffic; this is ARP poisoning/cache poisoning; the switch-level mitigation is Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) — the switch validates ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table and drops ARP packets that contain MAC-to-IP mappings inconsistent with known lease assignments
choice_c: ARP poisoning only works between directly connected hosts — it cannot affect routing through a default gateway
choice_d: Gratuitous ARP can only update the ARP cache if the target host previously sent an ARP request
explanation: ARP poisoning mechanics: attacker sends ARP reply: "192.168.1.1 is at AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF (attacker MAC)." All hosts receiving this overwrite their ARP cache. Subsequent traffic to internet → goes to attacker → attacker forwards to real router (transparent MITM). Mitigation: DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection): switch checks ARP sender IP:MAC against DHCP snooping table. If attacker claims 192.168.1.1 but DHCP table shows 192.168.1.1 belongs to CC:DD:EE:FF:00:11 → drop. Static entries needed for devices with static IPs.
aspect_key: resolve local destination mac for ipv4 communication

question: A network analyst captures traffic and observes that a connection shows SYN packets followed by SYN-ACK but then the client never sends the final ACK. Instead the client sends another SYN. What condition might explain this behavior and what network layer issue could cause it?
choice_a: Repeated SYN attempts mean the server is rejecting the connection because the client's credentials are invalid
choice_b: The most likely cause is that the SYN-ACK from the server is being dropped before reaching the client — possible causes include a stateful firewall that is only forwarding SYN packets outbound but dropping SYN-ACK responses (asymmetric routing causing half the path to miss the firewall state); packet loss on the return path; or the server's response is being filtered by a middlebox that permits outbound SYN but blocks or modifies inbound SYN-ACK; the client's TCP stack retransmits the SYN after the SYN-ACK wait timer expires; the server builds up half-open connections similar to a SYN flood
choice_c: Repeated SYN packets are normal TCP behavior — TCP always sends three SYNs before completing the handshake
choice_d: The client is deliberately refusing to complete the handshake as a port scanning technique
explanation: Asymmetric routing firewall problem: client → firewall A → server. Server → firewall B (different path) → client. Firewall A sees SYN; creates state. Firewall B has no state for this connection; drops SYN-ACK as "unsolicited inbound." Client never receives SYN-ACK; retransmits SYN. Diagnosis: traceroute from both directions; check firewall logs on both paths. Fix: ensure symmetric routing through the stateful firewall or use stateless ACLs for return traffic.
aspect_key: reliable sequencing; ack-driven retransmission; and handshake control

question: An organization uses NAT with a single public IP for 500 internal hosts. A security audit flags this as a concern for incident response. Explain the specific forensic problem this creates and the technical solution used by enterprises to address it.
choice_a: NAT creates no forensic problems — the public IP is always uniquely traceable to one internal host
choice_b: NAT hides all 500 internal hosts behind one public IP — when an external party reports malicious activity from that IP there is no way to identify which of the 500 internal hosts was responsible from the external log alone; the NAT translation table maps public-IP:port to private-IP:port — but this table is volatile and is only retained in memory; log entries must be timestamped and correlated with NAT logs to identify the source host; the technical solution is NAT logging (syslog of each translation entry with timestamp; private source IP; port; public port; protocol) and integration with a SIEM for correlation
choice_c: NAT logging is only possible on Linux-based NAT gateways — commercial firewalls do not log translations
choice_d: Since all 500 hosts share one public IP; each host's traffic is permanently mixed and cannot be separated
explanation: NAT forensics: external log: "203.0.113.1:12345 initiated attack at 14:32:15 UTC." Internal investigation: NAT device log at 14:32:15 → translation 203.0.113.1:12345 ↔ 192.168.1.100:54321 → attacker was 192.168.1.100. SIEM: correlates NAT log + DHCP log (who had 192.168.1.100 at that time) → identifies user. Requirements: (1) NAT logs must be preserved (correlation window). (2) DHCP logs must map IP→device. (3) Timestamps must be synchronized (NTP critical).
aspect_key: private-to-public translation for internet access

question: A packet has destination IP 10.5.10.200. Three routing table entries could match: 10.0.0.0/8; 10.5.0.0/16; and 10.5.10.0/24. Which route does the router use and why?
choice_a: The router uses the first matching route it finds — 10.0.0.0/8 is used because it appears first
choice_b: The router uses longest prefix match — 10.5.10.0/24 is used because it is the most specific match (24 bits of the destination IP match the route's prefix vs 16 bits for /16 and 8 bits for /8); longest prefix match always selects the most specific route regardless of the order entries appear in the routing table; this allows hierarchical routing where general routes handle traffic broadly and more specific routes override them for specific destinations
choice_c: All three routes match equally — the router load-balances across all three paths
choice_d: The router uses the route with the lowest administrative distance — this is always the /8 route
explanation: Longest prefix match: 10.5.10.200 binary prefix: 10.5.10 = matches /24 on first 24 bits. Also matches /16 on first 16 bits. Also matches /8 on first 8 bits. Most specific = /24 = 24-bit match wins. Use case: ISP has 10.0.0.0/8 summary route. Customer has 10.5.10.0/24 specific route. Packets to 10.5.10.200 → use /24 (customer). Packets to 10.7.0.0 → only /8 matches → use /8 (summary). This is how the internet routes traffic: specific beats general.
aspect_key: independent packet forwarding and possible out-of-order arrival

question: A technician reports that two hosts (10.1.1.50/25 and 10.1.1.200/25) cannot communicate even though they are physically on the same switch and both have correct IPs and masks. What is the exact cause and what is needed to fix it?
choice_a: The /25 mask cannot create two separate subnets — hosts with /25 always share one subnet
choice_b: The mask /25 divides 10.1.1.0/24 into two subnets: 10.1.1.0/25 (range .0-.127) and 10.1.1.128/25 (range .128-.255). Host .50 is in 10.1.1.0/25; host .200 is in 10.1.1.128/25 — they are on different subnets; even though they share a physical switch they are in different Layer 3 networks; each host determines the other is not local (their subnet ANDs differ) and attempts to route to it via the default gateway; if no default gateway is configured (or the gateway has no route between the two /25 subnets) communication fails; fix: add a router or Layer 3 switch with interfaces in both /25 subnets to provide inter-subnet routing
choice_c: Both hosts are in the same subnet because they are on the same physical switch — physical layer determines subnet membership
choice_d: The issue is a duplicate IP address — two hosts with addresses this close together indicate a conflict
explanation: Layer 3 vs Layer 2 separation: same physical switch ≠ same Layer 3 subnet. .50 AND 255.255.255.128 = 10.1.1.0. .200 AND 255.255.255.128 = 10.1.1.128. Different results → different subnets → routing required. Fix options: (1) Layer 3 switch (SVI): configure two SVIs 10.1.1.1/25 and 10.1.1.129/25 → enable ip routing. (2) External router: each subnet has an interface. (3) Change both hosts to /24 mask if subnetting is unintentional.
aspect_key: subnet mask and prefix split determine network id vs host space

question: An organization's web server is responding but the DNS name resolves to the wrong IP for some users. The server's A record was updated 6 hours ago from 1.2.3.4 to 5.6.7.8. Some users still reach 1.2.3.4 (the old server). What is the exact mechanism causing this and what could the administrator have done to prevent this issue?
choice_a: The web server's certificate is bound to 1.2.3.4 which is why some users are directed to it
choice_b: The old A record had a TTL that has not yet expired in the caches of affected users' resolvers — if the old TTL was 86400 seconds (24 hours) and the record was changed 6 hours ago those resolvers still have 18 hours of cached validity; they continue serving 1.2.3.4 to clients; the administrator could have prevented this by reducing the TTL to 300 seconds at least 24 hours before the planned IP change; low-TTL records expire quickly after the change allowing all resolvers to pick up the new IP within 5 minutes
choice_c: DNS records update instantly on all resolvers worldwide — the issue is the server itself sending old responses
choice_d: The TTL only affects caching within the same AS; international resolvers always check the authoritative server
explanation: Pre-migration TTL reduction protocol: Day 1 - reduce TTL from 86400 to 300 (5 min). Day 2 - change IP (all caches expire within 5 min). Day 3 - restore TTL to 86400. Without this: at 86400s TTL some resolvers cached the record just before the change → 24h of split-brain. With 300s TTL: maximum 5-minute split-brain window. 6h into migration with 24h old TTL: 18h remaining for stale caches. Users on resolvers that re-queried after the change see new IP.
aspect_key: records; zones; resolver behavior; and lookup workflows

question: A host's IP address is 172.31.200.50 with mask 255.255.192.0. Using binary analysis identify: the CIDR prefix length; the network address; the broadcast address; and whether the host 172.31.150.100 is in the same subnet.
choice_a: 172.31.200.50/18 is in the 172.31.192.0/18 subnet; 172.31.150.100 is in a different subnet
choice_b: Mask 255.255.192.0 = /18 (8+8+2 network bits). Third octet mask = 11000000 = 192. Third octet of 200: 11001000 AND 11000000 = 11000000 = 192. Network address = 172.31.192.0/18. Broadcast: last 14 bits = 1: third octet 192+63=255; fourth octet 255 → broadcast = 172.31.255.255. For 172.31.150.100: third octet 150 = 10010110 AND 11000000 = 10000000 = 128 → network = 172.31.128.0/18. Different network (128 ≠ 192) → different subnet → requires routing
choice_c: 255.255.192.0 is a /17 mask; the subnet increment in the third octet is 64
choice_d: 172.31.150.100 is in the same subnet because all 172.31.x.x addresses share the same /16 network
aspect_key: octet conversion between binary and decimal for subnet calculations

question: A network administrator uses the subnet 192.168.1.0/24 for a company with 4 departments needing 50; 20; 10; and 2 hosts respectively. Using VLSM assign the most efficient subnets. Show the mask and first/last host for each.
choice_a: All four departments should share a single /25 subnet because the total is less than 126 hosts
choice_b: Largest first: 50 hosts → needs 6 bits (/26; 64 addresses; 62 usable): 192.168.1.0/26 (hosts .1-.62). 20 hosts → needs 5 bits (/27; 32 addresses; 30 usable): 192.168.1.64/27 (hosts .65-.94). 10 hosts → needs 4 bits (/28; 16 addresses; 14 usable): 192.168.1.96/28 (hosts .97-.110). 2 hosts → needs 2 bits (/30; 4 addresses; 2 usable): 192.168.1.112/30 (hosts .113-.114). Total used: 64+32+16+4 = 116 of 256 addresses
choice_c: VLSM cannot be applied within a single /24 — each department needs its own /24 block
choice_d: 50 hosts requires a /25 because 2^7 = 128 which is the nearest power of 2 above 50
explanation: VLSM in /24: start at .0. 50 hosts: 6 host bits = /26 = 64 addresses. First host .1; last host .62; broadcast .63. 20 hosts: 5 host bits = /27 = 32 addresses. Start .64; first host .65; last .94; broadcast .95. 10 hosts: 4 bits = /28 = 16 addresses. Start .96; first .97; last .110; broadcast .111. 2 hosts: 2 bits = /30 = 4 addresses. Start .112; first .113; last .114; broadcast .115. Remaining .116-.255 = 140 addresses unassigned.
aspect_key: subnet mask and prefix split determine network id vs host space

question: A host receives an IP packet with the Don't Fragment (DF) bit set and the packet is too large for the outgoing interface's MTU. What does the router do; what ICMP message is generated; and why does blocking this ICMP message type cause a connectivity problem called "black hole routing"?
choice_a: The router fragments the packet anyway when the DF bit is set because the router's MTU takes precedence
choice_b: When a packet with DF=1 is too large for the outgoing link the router cannot fragment it — the router drops the packet and sends an ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (Destination Unreachable; Fragmentation Needed) message back to the source containing the maximum MTU that can be used; the source uses this to reduce its packet size (Path MTU Discovery); if a firewall blocks ICMP Type 3 Code 4 the source never learns the smaller MTU — it keeps sending oversized DF packets; the router keeps dropping them silently; the connection appears to hang with the first few small packets (ACKs) getting through while large data transfers fail — this is called an ICMP black hole
choice_c: The router reduces the TTL and retransmits on the smaller link automatically
choice_d: DF bit packets are forwarded regardless of MTU — the destination handles oversized frames by reassembling them
explanation: ICMP black hole symptoms: TCP three-way handshake succeeds (small packets). Large data transfer begins → oversized packets dropped silently. Session hangs; eventually TCP gives up. Fix: allow ICMP unreachable messages (Type 3 Code 4) through firewalls. Alternative: TCP MSS clamping → iptables -t mangle -A FORWARD -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN;RST SYN -j TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu forces MSS to PMTU regardless of ICMP.
aspect_key: ttl; protocol field; addressing; and fragmentation controls

question: An enterprise that runs a single large 172.16.0.0/16 flat network is experiencing broadcast storms during peak hours. A consultant recommends subnetting into /24 networks. Calculate the number of /24 subnets available; the reduction in broadcast domain size; and explain the broadcast-reduction mechanism at the router
choice_a: /24 subnetting increases the total number of broadcasts because there are now many more subnet broadcasts
choice_b: 172.16.0.0/16 provides 8 subnet bits (256 possible /24 subnets); the broadcast domain shrinks from 65534 hosts per domain (one /16) to 254 hosts per domain (each /24); router interfaces segment Layer 2 domains — an ARP broadcast or DHCP Discover sent in one /24 is blocked at the router interface; it does not propagate to other /24 segments; even if all 254 hosts in one /24 broadcast simultaneously only those 254 hosts are affected; vs the /16 scenario where all 65534 hosts are flooded simultaneously; this reduces broadcast CPU overhead on every host by ~99.6%
choice_c: Routers forward all broadcasts to all subnets so the total broadcast traffic actually increases
choice_d: The broadcast reduction only applies to multicast traffic — unicast and ARP are unaffected
explanation: Broadcast math: /16: one domain; 65534 hosts. One host sends ARP broadcast → 65533 other hosts interrupt their CPUs to process it. /24: 256 domains; 254 hosts each. Same ARP broadcast → only 253 other hosts affected. Broadcast traffic CPU overhead: each host processes every broadcast regardless of relevance. /24 segments: hosts only see broadcasts from their own 254-host domain. Router boundary: router does not forward Layer 2 broadcasts — hard wall between broadcast domains.
aspect_key: segment networks to control broadcast scope and improve address efficiency

question: A TCP connection is established and 10000 bytes are to be sent. The receiver's window size is 4000 bytes and the MSS is 1000 bytes. How many segments can the sender transmit before waiting for an ACK; what is the optimal throughput scenario; and what happens if a single segment is dropped?
choice_a: The sender transmits all 10000 bytes at once and waits for one final ACK
choice_b: Window = 4000 bytes; MSS = 1000 bytes. Sender can transmit 4000/1000 = 4 segments before waiting for an ACK. Optimal throughput: sender sends segments 1-4 (4000 bytes); receiver ACKs all four → sender can send segments 5-8 while receiving ACK for 1-4 (sliding window). If segment 3 is dropped: receiver gets segments 1; 2; 4 → sends duplicate ACKs for segment 2 (acknowledging through 2000; waiting for 3000); after 3 duplicate ACKs sender retransmits segment 3 only (fast retransmit) rather than waiting for a timeout; receiver buffers segment 4 and ACKs 4000 after receiving segment 3
choice_c: Each segment must be ACKed individually before the next can be sent
choice_d: If a segment is dropped TCP retransmits the entire 10000-byte transfer from the beginning
explanation: TCP windowing: cwnd and receive window control outstanding data. 4000-byte window → 4 segments in flight. Sliding window: as ACKs arrive; window slides forward. Duplicate ACK fast retransmit: 3 dup ACKs for seq 2000 → retransmit segment starting at 2001 immediately (no timeout wait). Timeout retransmit (slow path): if no ACK at all → wait RTO (retransmit timeout) → retransmit → cwnd reset to 1 MSS (slow start). Fast retransmit much better than timeout.
aspect_key: reliable sequencing; ack-driven retransmission; and handshake control

question: An IP packet travels from host A through 8 routers to host B. Each router must look up the destination IP in its routing table. Explain what information each router uses to make its forwarding decision and why the source IP address of the original packet is irrelevant to forwarding
choice_a: Routers use the source IP to decide which interface to forward the packet out of
choice_b: Each router performs a longest-prefix match lookup using only the destination IP address in the IP header — the routing table maps destination prefixes to next-hop IPs and outgoing interfaces; the source IP is irrelevant to the forwarding decision (it is only used for return traffic routing at the destination and for security policies); at each hop: extract destination IP; find matching route; determine next-hop IP; find the MAC of the next hop via ARP; re-encapsulate in a new frame with updated destination MAC and decremented TTL; send on the outgoing interface
choice_c: Routers use both source and destination IP to select routes for load balancing purposes
choice_d: The source IP is used to select the return path so that ACKs take the same route back
explanation: Per-hop forwarding: router receives frame → extracts IP packet → reads destination IP. Routing table lookup (LPM): finds longest prefix match → next-hop IP + outgoing interface. ARP: if next-hop MAC not in ARP cache → ARP request on outgoing interface. New frame: src MAC = router outgoing interface MAC; dst MAC = next-hop MAC. TTL decremented. Original source IP unchanged end-to-end (unless NAT). Return path: completely independent routing decision at each hop for the return journey.
aspect_key: independent packet forwarding and possible out-of-order arrival

question: A UDP-based DNS query is sent and no response is received after 2 seconds. Describe the exact behavior of the DNS client resolver — what it does next; how many retries occur; and what the user experiences if all servers fail.
choice_a: DNS clients wait indefinitely for a response because UDP has no timeout mechanism
choice_b: UDP provides no built-in retransmission so the DNS resolver implements application-layer retry logic — the client waits the configured timeout (typically 2 seconds); retransmits the query to the same server; if still no response it tries the secondary DNS server (if configured); repeats the timeout cycle for each configured server; after exhausting all servers with configured retry counts (typically 2-4 attempts) the resolver returns SERVFAIL to the application; the application receives a name resolution error; the browser shows "DNS_PROBE_FINISHED_NXDOMAIN" or similar; the user cannot load any web pages until DNS is restored
choice_c: UDP DNS queries are retried automatically by the IP layer — the application layer is not involved
choice_d: The DNS client switches to TCP automatically after the first timeout and TCP handles retransmission
explanation: DNS client retry sequence: primary server 8.8.8.8 → timeout 2s → retry → timeout 2s. Secondary server 1.1.1.1 → timeout 2s → retry → timeout 2s. After all fail → SERVFAIL. Total wait = (2s × 2 retries × 2 servers) = 8 seconds before failure. User experience: browser hangs for 8 seconds then shows "This site can't be reached." All applications needing external names fail. Internal names (cached or answered by still-reachable internal DNS) may still work. Diagnostic: nslookup test.example.com → shows which server is failing.
aspect_key: connectionless low-overhead delivery with no reliability guarantees

question: What is the specific reason why private RFC 1918 IP addresses would cause problems if they appeared as source addresses in packets on the public internet — beyond simply "not being routable"?
choice_a: Private IPs cause packet loops on the internet because every router tries to forward them internally
choice_b: Three specific problems: (1) ambiguity — millions of organizations use 192.168.1.1 as their default gateway; a packet on the internet from 192.168.1.1 could claim to originate from any of them making attribution impossible; (2) anti-spoofing filters (BCP 38) — ISPs filter packets with RFC 1918 source addresses as they are definitionally spoofed (private addresses cannot originate from the internet); (3) return path failure — even if the packet reaches a destination the destination cannot route a reply to 192.168.1.1 because no public route exists for that prefix; the packet would be dropped or sent to the wrong network
choice_c: Private IP packets are blocked because they are too large for internet routing tables
choice_d: ISPs only block Class A private addresses (10.0.0.0/8); Class B and C private ranges are routable
explanation: Anti-spoofing enforcement: BCP 38 (RFC 2827) recommends ISPs configure ingress filtering — drop packets from customer connections whose source IP is not within the customer's allocated public prefix. Private source IPs from customers are dropped. Routing problem: if 192.168.1.1 packet reached a router with a default route; it would be forwarded toward the default → eventually discarded. Return traffic: no public route for 192.168.0.0/16 → routers would apply default route or drop.
aspect_key: routable internet space vs rfc1918 internal ranges

question: A system administrator wants to verify the exact path a packet takes to reach 8.8.8.8 and measure the latency at each hop. They run traceroute (or tracert on Windows). Explain the specific mechanism traceroute uses to discover each hop including the role of TTL; ICMP; and what information each probe reveals.
choice_a: Traceroute works by sending large packets that exceed the MTU of each router causing ICMP Fragmentation Needed messages that reveal the router's IP
choice_b: Traceroute sends a series of UDP probes (Linux) or ICMP Echo Requests (Windows tracert) with incrementally increasing TTL values — TTL=1: first router decrements to 0; drops the packet; sends ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11 Code 0) back to the source revealing hop 1's IP. TTL=2: second router sends ICMP Time Exceeded revealing hop 2. This continues until the destination responds with ICMP Port Unreachable (Linux; UDP to closed port) or ICMP Echo Reply (Windows). Each hop's round-trip time is measured three times. The result is a hop-by-hop map of the path with latency
choice_c: Traceroute uses ARP to discover the MAC address of each router hop along the path
choice_d: Traceroute requires SNMP access to each router — it queries the routing table directly
explanation: Traceroute probe detail: Linux: UDP packets to destination port 33434+ (unlikely to be open). Destination: ICMP Port Unreachable (3/3) → probe complete. Windows (tracert): ICMP Echo Request. Destination: ICMP Echo Reply → probe complete. Three probes per TTL → three RTT values displayed. * symbol: router does not respond to ICMP (firewall) or ICMP TTL Exceeded is rate-limited. Asymmetric paths: outbound path shown; return path unknown.
aspect_key: ttl; protocol field; addressing; and fragmentation controls

question: Two applications run on the same server: a web server on port 80 and a DNS server on port 53. Both receive traffic from the same client IP (192.168.1.50). How does the server's OS differentiate incoming packets destined for each service and deliver them to the correct application process?
choice_a: The server checks the TTL field in the IP header to determine which application should receive each packet
choice_b: The OS uses the 4-tuple (source IP; source port; destination IP; destination port) to demultiplex packets to the correct application — both services share the server's IP but run on different ports; an HTTP packet with destination port 80 is handed to the web server process; a DNS packet with destination port 53 is handed to the DNS service; the OS network stack uses a socket binding model where each application registers with the OS to receive traffic for a specific port (web server: bind 0.0.0.0:80; DNS: bind 0.0.0.0:53); incoming packets are matched to sockets by destination port
choice_c: Applications on the same server cannot share the same IP — each service must use a separate IP address
choice_d: The server reads the URL in the HTTP header to determine which application should handle each request
explanation: Socket demultiplexing: IP layer extracts Protocol field → TCP=6 or UDP=17. TCP/UDP layer reads destination port. Socket table lookup: port 80 → web server process (PID 1234). Port 53 → named/bind (PID 5678). OS kernel passes packet data to the appropriate socket buffer. Application reads from its socket buffer. Port conflict: if two applications try to bind the same port → "address already in use" error. This model supports thousands of simultaneous services on one IP.
aspect_key: tcp/udp port numbers identify application endpoints

question: A classful Class B network 172.20.0.0/16 is being subdivided to create exactly 100 subnets each large enough for 200 hosts. What prefix length is required; how many subnets does it create; and does this satisfy both constraints simultaneously?
choice_a: A /24 creates 256 subnets of 254 hosts — but 256 > 100 so this over-subnets and should not be used
choice_b: 100 subnets requires at least 7 subnet bits (2^7 = 128 ≥ 100; 2^6 = 64 < 100); 200 hosts requires at least 8 host bits (2^8 - 2 = 254 ≥ 200); Class B /16 base + 8 subnet bits = /24; host bits = 8 → 254 usable hosts per subnet (satisfies ≥200); subnets = 2^8 = 256 ≥ 100 (satisfies ≥100); the /24 mask satisfies both constraints — creating 256 subnets of 254 hosts each; all 100 required subnets can be allocated
choice_c: 7 subnet bits + 9 host bits = /23 which provides 512 hosts per subnet satisfying the 200-host requirement more efficiently
choice_d: A /25 provides 128 subnets of 126 hosts — 126 < 200 so it fails the host requirement
explanation: Constraint resolution: need ≥100 subnets AND ≥200 hosts/subnet. /24 (8+8): subnets=2^8=256 ✓ (>100); hosts=2^8-2=254 ✓ (>200). /23 (8+7): subnets=2^7=128 ✓; hosts=2^9-2=510 ✓. Both work; /24 uses the /16 efficiently — 256×256=65536 addresses = entire /16 used. /23 would need 100×512=51200 addresses. Use /24 for cleaner design; /23 if more host growth is expected.
aspect_key: a/b/c defaults as historical foundation for subnet logic

question: A network engineer discovers that a host on 10.0.0.0/8 cannot communicate with a host on 10.0.0.0/24 on the same physical segment. Both have the IP address 10.0.0.50 but different masks. What is the root cause and what does the subnet mask difference cause?
choice_a: Both hosts can communicate because they share the same IP address — IP addressing does not include the mask
choice_b: Two hosts with the same IP address (10.0.0.50) on the same segment creates an IP address conflict — ARP responses from both NICs claim the same IP causing the ARP cache on other hosts to flip between the two MACs intermittently; additionally the mask mismatch means the /8 host considers any 10.x.x.x address local (ARPs directly) while the /24 host only considers 10.0.0.x local (uses gateway for others); the network administrator must assign unique IPs to both hosts; the mask mismatch compounds the conflict but the duplicate IP is the primary cause of communication failure
choice_c: IP address conflicts are harmless as long as the subnet masks are different
choice_d: Hosts with different subnet masks can share an IP because the mask makes them appear to be on different networks
explanation: Duplicate IP effects: ARP conflict. Host A sends ARP for 10.0.0.50 → both conflicting hosts reply. ARP cache at other hosts: oscillates between the two MACs. Connections to 10.0.0.50 intermittently reach either host. OS detection: Windows sends gratuitous ARP on boot — if another reply is received it displays "IP address conflict" in the system tray. Mask mismatch secondary effect: /8 host ARPs for any 10.x.x.x → may not route through the gateway when it should. Fix: assign unique IPs; then standardize mask configuration.
aspect_key: host bits all 0 represent subnet identifier

question: Using CIDR supernetting: a company has four contiguous /24 networks (203.0.112.0/24; 203.0.113.0/24; 203.0.114.0/24; 203.0.115.0/24). Can these be summarized into a single prefix? Show the binary work to verify and give the summary prefix.
choice_a: These four /24 networks cannot be summarized because they have different third-octet values
choice_b: Converting third octets to binary: 112=01110000; 113=01110001; 114=01110010; 115=01110011. All share the same first 6 bits (011100) — the 7th and 8th bits differ (00; 01; 10; 11). The common prefix is the first 22 bits (16 bits for first two octets + 6 bits of third octet = /22). Summary prefix: 203.0.112.0/22. This single entry covers all four /24 networks; an ISP advertising this route instead of four /24 entries reduces the routing table by 3 entries
choice_c: Four /24 networks can only be summarized if the first two octets also differ
choice_d: These can be summarized as 203.0.0.0/16 which is the smallest block containing all four
explanation: CIDR summarization rule: networks must be contiguous AND the block must align to a power-of-2 boundary. 4 networks: need /22 (2^2 = 4). Check: 112 = 0111 0000. Starting at /22: first 6 bits of third octet = 011100. All four third octets (112-115) share 011100__ pattern → valid /22 block. 203.0.112.0/22 covers 203.0.112.0 through 203.0.115.255. BGP: single /22 advertisement instead of four /24 advertisements.
aspect_key: classless prefix-length notation enables flexible subnet sizing

question: An encapsulation trace shows a 1500-byte Ethernet payload. A security analyst calculates: 20-byte IP header + 20-byte TCP header + 1460-byte payload. The analyst wants to add an application-layer header of 50 bytes without changing the Ethernet frame size. Explain what must change and why.
choice_a: The analyst can simply add 50 more bytes — Ethernet frames can stretch to accommodate any payload
choice_b: The application-layer header (50 bytes) increases the application data by 50 bytes without reducing the TCP payload — the TCP payload is 1460 bytes leaving room for 1460 bytes of application data; adding a 50-byte application header reduces the application data by 50 bytes to 1410 bytes but the total TCP payload remains 1460 bytes (50 header + 1410 data); the Ethernet frame stays at 1500 bytes; what changes is the available space for application data — the TCP payload is fixed by MTU; each header added at any layer consumes space from the payload of that layer's encapsulation
choice_c: Each layer's header is added before the Ethernet frame size is calculated — all headers are overhead on top of the MTU
choice_d: The TCP MSS must be reduced to 1460 bytes to accommodate the new application header
explanation: MTU math: Ethernet payload = 1500 bytes (standard MTU). IP header = 20 bytes → IP payload = 1480 bytes. TCP header = 20 bytes → TCP payload = 1460 bytes = MSS (Maximum Segment Size). Application header = 50 bytes → application data = 1410 bytes per segment. MSS negotiation: MSS = MTU - IP header - TCP header = 1500-20-20 = 1460. Application overhead reduces effective application throughput: 1460 bytes available; 50 bytes consumed by header; 1410 bytes actual data per segment.
aspect_key: application data wrapped through transport; network; and data-link layers

question: A network administrator needs to understand why pinging 192.168.1.255 on a /24 subnet causes all hosts on the subnet to respond but pinging 192.168.1.256 returns an error immediately without any response. Explain both behaviors precisely.
choice_a: 255 is a special subnet ID reserved for routers; 256 causes an overflow in the octet value
choice_b: 192.168.1.255 is the directed broadcast for 192.168.1.0/24 (all host bits = 1 in the last octet) — an ICMP Echo Request sent to this address is delivered to all hosts on the subnet; each host that has ICMP reply enabled sends an ICMP Echo Reply back to the sender; 192.168.1.256 is an invalid IPv4 address — each octet must be 0-255 (8 bits maximum); 256 cannot be represented in 8 bits (binary overflow) and the OS immediately rejects the command-line input before sending any packet
choice_c: 192.168.1.255 is rejected by the router while .256 is forwarded but causes ICMP Port Unreachable
choice_d: Directed broadcasts to .255 are blocked by all routers to prevent smurf amplification attacks
explanation: Directed broadcast security: smurf attack — attacker sends ICMP Echo to directed broadcast with spoofed source IP → all hosts on subnet reply → victim receives amplified flood. Modern routers: "no ip directed-broadcast" configured by default on Cisco (since IOS 12.0) — drops directed broadcasts arriving from outside. Within-subnet broadcast ping: still works locally if host ICMP is not filtered. 256 error: OS validates address format before socket creation → invalid range → immediate error.
aspect_key: host bits all 1 represent one-to-all delivery in subnet

question: A security review finds that an internal application sends data using UDP without any error detection at the application layer. The developer argues that the UDP checksum provides sufficient error detection. A security engineer disagrees. Who is correct and why?
choice_a: The security engineer is wrong — the UDP checksum covers the entire packet and is always sufficient
choice_b: The security engineer is correct — the UDP checksum is optional in IPv4 (a sender can set it to zero to disable it) and only covers the UDP header and data payload; the checksum is a 16-bit one's complement sum which detects many but not all errors — it can miss certain multi-bit error patterns; for security-critical applications the application layer should add its own integrity mechanism (HMAC; TLS; checksums over stored data); additionally the UDP checksum protects against accidental data corruption not intentional manipulation — an attacker could modify both the data and the checksum simultaneously making UDP checksum insufficient for security
choice_c: UDP checksum verification is performed by hardware and is therefore always correct
choice_d: UDP checksum errors are automatically corrected by the receiving NIC before the OS processes the data
explanation: UDP checksum limitations: (1) Optional in IPv4 (zero = disabled). (2) Detects single-bit and some multi-bit errors but not all patterns. (3) Not a cryptographic hash — trivially forgeable. (4) No authentication — cannot verify sender. Application-layer integrity: TLS provides authentication + encryption + integrity (HMAC) over the data. Application checksum: if TLS is not used; applications should add their own integrity check. Critical data: databases; file transfers should always verify end-to-end integrity regardless of transport checksums.
aspect_key: checksum (transport/header) and crc/fcs (frame integrity)

question: A home router uses NAT so several internal devices can share one public IPv4 address on the internet. An internal laptop opens a web session to an internet server and the server's reply packet later returns to the router's public IP address. How does the router know which internal laptop should receive that returning reply traffic?
choice_a: The router checks its NAT translation or state table to match the returning public address and port to the original internal private address and port
choice_b: The router sends an ARP broadcast on the LAN to ask which device opened the internet session
choice_c: The router forwards the reply to every internal host until one host accepts it
choice_d: The router chooses the internal host whose private IP address is numerically closest to the destination port
explanation: For internet return traffic NAT works by keeping a translation or session-state entry that maps the internal private address and source port to the translated public address and port. When the reply comes back to the public IP and translated port the router matches that entry and forwards the packet to the correct internal host. ARP is only used on the local link to learn MAC addresses and does not identify which internal host should receive internet return traffic through NAT.
aspect_key: return traffic matched by nat translation state

question: A network student calculates that a /19 subnet should provide 8190 usable hosts. The student's calculation is 2^13 - 2 = 8190. A colleague says the student made an error. Who is correct and show the verification.
choice_a: The student made an error — a /19 subnet only provides 4094 usable hosts because the prefix is 19 not 13
choice_b: The student is correct — a /19 leaves 32-19=13 host bits; 2^13 = 8192 addresses; subtract 2 (network + broadcast) = 8190 usable hosts; the colleague is wrong; the calculation 2^(32-prefix) - 2 is the correct formula for usable hosts in any IPv4 subnet
choice_c: A /19 subnet provides 2^19 = 524288 usable hosts because the prefix is 19
choice_d: The formula 2^n - 2 does not apply to subnets larger than /24 — a different formula is needed
explanation: Verification: /19 prefix → host bits = 32-19 = 13. 2^13 = 8192 total addresses. Usable = 8192-2 = 8190. Subnet range example: 10.0.0.0/19 → hosts 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.31.254 → broadcast 10.0.31.255. Check: 32 × 256 = 8192 addresses (third octet changes by 32 for each /19). Colleague was wrong. Formula 2^(32-prefix) - 2 is universal for all IPv4 prefix lengths from /1 to /30.
aspect_key: 2^n - 2 model for assignable hosts in subnet

question: A packet capture shows a TCP session where data flows normally for 50 seconds then suddenly stops. The last segment seen is from the server and no further packets appear for 90 seconds. Then a TCP RST appears from the client. Explain what most likely happened during those 90 seconds of silence and what the RST indicates.
choice_a: The 90 seconds of silence means the network link failed and was restored automatically
choice_b: The most likely scenario is TCP keepalive or application-layer timeout — after the last server segment; the client had no more data to send and the application was idle; during the 90-second silence: either no application activity occurred (normal for idle connection); or the client sent keepalive probes (TCP keepalive default: 2-hour idle + 10 probes at 75-second intervals); after the 90 seconds the client application either timed out or was forcibly closed by the OS — the RST is the client terminating the connection immediately rather than using a graceful FIN handshake; this suggests the application closed abruptly (crash; timeout; or explicit close without draining)
choice_c: Silence in a TCP capture always indicates packet loss — the 90 seconds are retransmissions that were not captured
choice_d: A RST after silence means a firewall injected the RST to terminate an idle connection exceeding an inactivity timer
explanation: Connection silence analysis: normal TCP: both sides idle → no packets → silence is expected (TCP is bidirectional; no data = no traffic). RST vs FIN: FIN = graceful close (data drained); RST = abrupt termination. RST after 90s silence: likely the application called close()/shutdown() on the socket. OS detected the connection was still open (no FIN exchange yet) → sends RST. Firewall scenario: corporate firewalls often send RST to both sides of idle connections exceeding inactivity limits (common: 30-3600 seconds).
aspect_key: reliable sequencing; ack-driven retransmission; and handshake control

question: A network engineer must summarize the four subnets 10.4.0.0/24; 10.4.1.0/24; 10.4.2.0/24; and 10.4.3.0/24 into a single route. Show the binary work for the third octet to determine the common prefix and give the summary address and prefix length.
choice_a: The four /24 subnets can be summarized as 10.4.0.0/16 because they all begin with 10.4
choice_b: Third octet values in binary: 0=00000000; 1=00000001; 2=00000010; 3=00000011. All four share the same first 6 bits (000000) — the 7th and 8th bits differ (00; 01; 10; 11). The common prefix across all four includes the first two full octets (16 bits) plus 6 bits of the third octet = 22 bits total. Summary route: 10.4.0.0/22. This covers all four /24 networks exactly without including any other networks (10.4.0.0-10.4.3.255)
choice_c: The summary should be 10.4.0.0/23 because that covers two /24 blocks and these blocks would need two /23 entries
choice_d: Third octet 0-3 requires a /24 summary mask because the values differ in the last 2 bits
explanation: Bit analysis: 0=000000|00; 1=000000|01; 2=000000|10; 3=000000|11. Common prefix ends at bit 6 (6th bit from the left = 0 for all four). 16 (two octets) + 6 bits = /22. /22 block size = 2^(32-22) = 1024 addresses = 4 × 256. Covers 10.4.0.0 to 10.4.3.255. Verify: 10.4.4.0 would be the next /22 block. This is the minimal summary that covers exactly these four /24s.
aspect_key: octet conversion between binary and decimal for subnet calculations

question: An administrator is asked to design a subnet for a network where each subnet must have exactly 14 usable host addresses. Show the required prefix length; the subnet mask in dotted decimal; and explain why a /28 is chosen rather than a /27.
choice_a: A /27 provides exactly 14 usable hosts because 2^5 - 2 = 30 which rounds down to 14
choice_b: 14 usable hosts requires the smallest prefix where 2^n - 2 ≥ 14 → n=4: 2^4-2=14 (exactly meets requirement). Host bits = 4 → prefix = 32-4 = /28. Subnet mask = 255.255.255.240 (last octet: 11110000 = 240). A /27 would provide 2^5-2=30 hosts per subnet which exceeds the requirement — it wastes 16 addresses per subnet; when designing for exactly 14 hosts the /28 provides the tightest fit.
choice_c: A /28 creates subnets of 12 usable hosts because the network and broadcast addresses must be subtracted from 14
choice_d: The /28 subnet provides 14 hosts but requires a Class C address for this calculation
explanation: Tight-fit subnet design: 14 hosts needed. 2^3-2=6 (too few). 2^4-2=14 (exact fit). /28. Mask: 11110000 = 240 → 255.255.255.240. Subnets per /24: 256/16 = 16 possible /28 subnets. If /27 used: 30 hosts available → 16 unused. In VLSM design: using the smallest fitting subnet minimizes address waste. Example /28 subnet: 192.168.1.16/28 → network .16; usable .17-.30; broadcast .31.
aspect_key: 2^n - 2 model for assignable hosts in subnet

question: A CRC error is detected in an Ethernet frame. Explain the complete mechanism by which the receiver detects the error; why the frame is simply dropped rather than retransmitted at Layer 2; and which higher-layer protocol is responsible for recovery.
choice_a: Layer 2 retransmits the corrupted frame automatically using a built-in ARQ mechanism
choice_b: The receiver recalculates the CRC over the received frame and compares it to the FCS value in the trailer — if they differ a bit was corrupted in transit and the frame is silently discarded; Ethernet at Layer 2 has no acknowledgement or retransmission mechanism (it was designed this way deliberately because the original coaxial bus media was reliable enough that errors were rare and adding ARQ overhead to every frame was not worth the cost); higher layer protocols provide recovery: if the frame contained a TCP segment TCP detects the missing data via a gap in sequence numbers and requests retransmission from the source; if UDP is used the UDP application must implement its own recovery or tolerate the loss
choice_c: The receiver sends a NACK message to the source requesting retransmission of the corrupted frame
choice_d: CRC errors trigger a network-wide ARP broadcast to reroute traffic around the corrupted link
explanation: CRC calculation: both sender and receiver calculate CRC using the same polynomial (IEEE 802.3: CRC-32). Sender appends CRC. Receiver recalculates over received data. Match → valid. Mismatch → corrupt → discard. Layer 2 no-ARQ design: Ethernet runs over reliable point-to-point links; error rates typically <10^-10. Adding ARQ adds 2× overhead (ACK + retransmit window). TCP handles errors at Layer 4 → no need to duplicate at Layer 2. Wireless (802.11): uses ARQ (block ACK) because wireless error rates are much higher.
aspect_key: checksum (transport/header) and crc/fcs (frame integrity)

question: A technician is asked to subnet 192.168.0.0/24 to accommodate 5 separate departments needing 30; 25; 20; 10; and 6 hosts respectively. Without using more than one /24 show that VLSM allows all five to fit and calculate total address usage vs the /24 total.
choice_a: VLSM cannot fit five different-sized subnets into a single /24 — separate /24 blocks are required
choice_b: All five departments fit within 192.168.0.0/24 using VLSM: Dept 1 (30 hosts) → /27 (62 usable): .0/27 → 32 addresses. Dept 2 (25 hosts) → /27 (62 usable): .32/27 → 32 addresses. Dept 3 (20 hosts) → /27 (62 usable): .64/27 → 32 addresses. Dept 4 (10 hosts) → /28 (14 usable): .96/28 → 16 addresses. Dept 5 (6 hosts) → /29 (6 usable — exactly meets requirement): .112/29 → 8 addresses. Total used: 32+32+32+16+8 = 120 of 256 → 136 addresses remaining unallocated within the /24
choice_c: VLSM requires departments to be separated into different /24 blocks to avoid fragmentation
choice_d: The smallest /29 provides only 4 usable hosts — 6 hosts requires a /28 for this department
explanation: VLSM allocation: Dept 1: 30 hosts → 5 bits → /27 (30 usable). .0-.31. Dept 2: 25 hosts → /27 (30 usable). .32-.63. Dept 3: 20 hosts → /27 (30 usable). .64-.95. Dept 4: 10 hosts → /28 (14 usable). .96-.111. Dept 5: 6 hosts → 3 host bits → /29 (6 usable: 2^3-2=6). .112-.119. Verify: /29 = 6 usable (exactly meets 6-host requirement — valid!). Remaining: .120-.255 = 136 addresses for future allocation. Total waste: only the excess per subnet.
aspect_key: subnet mask and prefix split determine network id vs host space

question: When a host starts with empty ARP and DNS caches, what sequence of ARP, DNS, and TCP events is required to load a webpage by hostname?
choice_a: Booting a host automatically populates all ARP; DNS; and TCP caches so no lookups are needed
choice_b: Complete sequence from cold cache: (1) Host needs to reach www.example.com. (2) DNS lookup: host sends UDP packet to DNS server — first ARPs for default gateway MAC (DNS server is on a different subnet). (3) ARP for gateway: broadcasts ARP request for 192.168.1.1. Gateway replies. (4) DNS query sent to gateway → forwarded to DNS. DNS resolver queries root → TLD → authoritative → returns 93.184.216.34. (5) TCP SYN to 93.184.216.34: host checks routing table — not local → send to gateway. MAC already cached from step 3. (6) SYN-ACK received → ACK sent (three-way handshake). (7) HTTP/HTTPS GET request sent. (8) Response received — webpage loads.
choice_c: The host can reach www.example.com without ARP because IP routing handles all address translation
choice_d: DNS is never needed if the host uses a direct IP address instead of a hostname — the browser automatically converts hostnames to IPs
explanation: Cold cache sequence detail: (1) ARP for default gateway (must happen before DNS query can be sent outside the subnet). (2) DNS: recursive query via gateway. DNS resolver may need to ARP for its own gateway. (3) IP returned. (4) ARP for gateway again (cached from step 1 — no re-ARP needed). (5) TCP SYN routed via gateway. (6) Handshake completes. (7) GET request. Total pre-page lookups: 1 ARP + 1 DNS (3 DNS sub-queries at resolver) + 1 TCP handshake. HTTPS adds TLS handshake after step 6.
aspect_key: resolve local destination mac for ipv4 communication

question: An organization gets two ISP connections and wants to use them simultaneously for outbound internet traffic. Explain why NAT complicates this multi-homed design and what specific mechanism is needed to ensure return traffic uses the same ISP it exited from.
choice_a: Multiple ISP connections work automatically with NAT — traffic distributes evenly on both links
choice_b: With two ISPs (ISP1 public IP 1.1.1.1; ISP2 public IP 2.2.2.2): each outbound connection is NATted to the source ISP's public IP; return traffic from the internet is directed to the public IP in the packet (1.1.1.1 or 2.2.2.2); but the router must ensure each ISP's traffic returns via the same ISP that the outbound NAT used; if the router sends outbound via ISP1 and NATted to 1.1.1.1 but return traffic somehow comes back via ISP2 the router has no NAT state for the return session via ISP2 and drops it; the mechanism needed is Policy-Based Routing (PBR) combined with ISP-specific NAT pools: traffic leaving via ISP1 is NATted to 1.1.1.1; ISP2 traffic to 2.2.2.2; and PBR routes the return traffic to the correct ISP interface
choice_c: Return traffic is handled automatically by OSPF between the two ISPs
choice_d: Multi-homed NAT only requires two default routes — the router automatically matches NAT state to return traffic
explanation: Multi-ISP NAT design: NAT state entry: 192.168.1.50:52300 → 1.1.1.1:10001 via ISP1. Return: internet sends to 1.1.1.1:10001 → must arrive via ISP1. Problem: if ISP1 goes down → some connections fail (NAT state references down link). PBR: route packets by source IP to specific ISP + apply matching NAT pool. Advanced: BGP multi-homing — advertise both IP blocks via both ISPs; BGP handles return-path symmetry. Simple failover: DHCP-learned default route via healthy ISP.
aspect_key: private-to-public translation for internet access

question: If a DNS zone delegates to a name server whose A record is inside the same delegated zone, what circular lookup problem occurs, and how is that problem solved?
choice_a: NS records and their A records must always be in different zones — this is why the problem cannot occur
choice_b: This is the "glue record" problem — when a resolver asks the root for example.com the root returns NS record: "ns1.example.com" — but to contact ns1.example.com the resolver needs ns1.example.com's IP address which is in the example.com zone that it is trying to query; this circular dependency is resolved by "glue records" — the parent zone (root or TLD registry) includes an additional A record for ns1.example.com in its response along with the NS record so the resolver has the IP to contact the authoritative server without needing to first resolve it
choice_c: The resolver caches all NS records permanently to avoid this circular dependency
choice_d: NS records are always IP addresses not hostnames so the glue record problem cannot occur
explanation: Glue records: root returns for example.com: AUTHORITY section: "NS ns1.example.com." ADDITIONAL section: "A 93.184.216.100" (glue). Without glue: resolver can't reach ns1.example.com (needs example.com to be resolved to find ns1's IP → circular). Registrar management: when registering ns1.example.com as nameserver for example.com the registrar requires the IP address → stores as glue in the TLD zone. Out-of-zone nameservers (ns1.otherdomain.com) don't need glue — they're resolvable via their own domain's delegation.
aspect_key: records; zones; resolver behavior; and lookup workflows

question: Calculate the binary representation of the subnet mask for a /20 network; convert it to dotted decimal; and determine how many /24 subnets fit within a single /20 block. Then identify the network address of 10.16.50.0/20.
choice_a: A /20 mask is 255.255.240.0 and contains 16 /24 subnets; 10.16.50.0/20's network is 10.16.48.0
choice_b: Binary: 11111111.11111111.11110000.00000000 = 255.255.240.0 = /20 (20 ones). Third octet mask = 11110000 = 240. The 4 host bits in the third octet provide a subnet increment of 2^4 = 16 in the third octet. /20 contains 2^4 = 16 /24 subnets (each /24 adds 1 bit; 20→24 = 4 more bits = 16 subnets per /20). Network of 10.16.50.0/20: third octet 50 AND 240: 50=00110010 AND 11110000=00110000=48. Network = 10.16.48.0/20. Contains /24s from 10.16.48.0/24 through 10.16.63.0/24
choice_c: A /20 creates 8 /24 subnets because the bit difference between /20 and /24 is 4 bits = 16 total but half are reserved
choice_d: The network address of 10.16.50.0/20 is 10.16.50.0 because the last two octets are both zero
explanation: /20 analysis: 20 bits network → third octet has 4 network bits (11110000) + 8 bits fourth octet = all host. Increment in third octet = 2^4=16 (value of lowest network bit). /20 block size: 16×256 = 4096 addresses. /24 blocks per /20: 16 (one per third-octet increment). 10.16.50.0: third octet 50 AND 240 = 48. /20 range: 10.16.48.0 to 10.16.63.255. 16 /24 subnets: .48.0/24 through .63.0/24.
aspect_key: octet conversion between binary and decimal for subnet calculations

question: An IP packet arrives at a router with Protocol field value 17. The router's ACL is configured to block Protocol 6. Is this packet blocked? Explain how the Protocol field value determines the Layer 4 behavior and what application traffic typically uses Protocol 17.
choice_a: Protocol 6 and Protocol 17 are both used for HTTP — the router should block both
choice_b: Protocol 17 = UDP (IANA-assigned). Protocol 6 = TCP. The ACL is configured to block Protocol 6 (TCP) — Protocol 17 (UDP) is not blocked; the packet passes. Protocol 17 tells the receiving host to pass the payload to the UDP stack; typical traffic using UDP (Protocol 17) includes DNS (port 53); DHCP (67/68); NTP (123); SNMP (161); TFTP (69); and real-time audio/video (RTP). Protocol field is in the IP header — the ACL inspects it at Layer 3 before any port-based filtering
choice_c: Protocol 17 is ICMP — the packet carries a ping or traceroute message
choice_d: Protocol 17 is blocked because it is in the same protocol family as TCP (Protocol 6)
explanation: Protocol field reference: 1=ICMP; 2=IGMP; 6=TCP; 17=UDP; 41=IPv6-in-IPv4; 47=GRE; 50=ESP; 89=OSPF. ACL example: "deny ip protocol 6" → blocks all TCP. "permit ip protocol 17" → allows UDP. Port-based filtering requires Protocol=6 or 17 first then port inspection. Protocol 17 UDP passes → application at server inspects destination port. DNS on UDP/53: this packet would reach the DNS server if destination port 53 is not additionally filtered.
aspect_key: ttl; protocol field; addressing; and fragmentation controls

question: A host running Windows shows IP address 169.254.1.100 after booting. The administrator verifies the DHCP server is running and responds to pings from other subnets. What are the three most likely specific causes to check in order and what commands verify each?
choice_a: The DHCP server being pingable from other subnets proves it is working — the host must have a bad NIC
choice_b: Three most likely causes in order: (1) IP helper address missing on the VLAN interface — if no DHCP relay is configured the DHCP Discover broadcast cannot reach the server on a different subnet; verify: check Layer 3 switch or router "show running-config interface vlan X" for "ip helper-address." (2) Wrong VLAN assignment — if the host's switch port is in the wrong VLAN the DHCP traffic never reaches the correct relay; verify: "show interfaces switchport" on the switch port. (3) DHCP scope has no addresses for this subnet — the server is running but has no scope for VLAN X; verify: DHCP console → check scope for the host's subnet; "show ip dhcp binding" shows zero entries for that range
choice_c: The 169.254.x.x address means the NIC is defective and must be replaced
choice_d: If pings work from other subnets the host's DHCP problem is a Windows OS configuration issue unrelated to networking
explanation: APIPA troubleshooting order: most-to-least likely. (1) Missing helper — most common in multi-VLAN environments. (2) VLAN misconfiguration — access port in wrong VLAN → broadcasts go to wrong DHCP scope. (3) No scope — server up but no scope for this subnet → all Discovers rejected. Less common: (4) DHCP scope exhausted. (5) Host NIC driver issue (rare; check by trying ipconfig /renew manually). Diagnostic shortcut: ipconfig /renew on host → watch error message for clues.
aspect_key: independent packet forwarding and possible out-of-order arrival

question: An organization's existing network 192.168.0.0/24 has grown to need an additional 300 hosts but the /24 only supports 254. The administrator suggests changing the mask to /23. Explain the specific impact of this change on all existing hosts and what reconfiguration is required.
choice_a: Changing to /23 expands the address space to 510 hosts but all existing hosts automatically inherit the new mask
choice_b: Changing the mask from /24 to /23 on the router does not automatically reconfigure any host — each host still has its old /24 mask; the router now routes the combined 192.168.0.0/23 block (covering .0.0 to .1.255) but hosts with /24 masks will not ARP for addresses in 192.168.1.x (they believe 192.168.1.x is on a different subnet and will route to the gateway instead of using direct ARP); to fix this every host must be reconfigured with /23 (either via updated DHCP scope or manual change); the gateway/router interface also needs the /23 mask; the transition requires coordinating all host reconfiguration to avoid partial-mask mismatches during the cutover
choice_c: Changing the subnet mask on the router automatically propagates to all hosts via ARP gratuitous broadcasts
choice_d: The /23 change can be applied incrementally — half the hosts can use /24 and half can use /23 without issues
explanation: /23 expansion impact: router: update interface to 192.168.0.1/23. DHCP: update scope to 192.168.0.0/23 mask + new range 192.168.1.1-254. Existing hosts: still configured /24. Host at .0.50/24: cannot ARP for 192.168.1.100 (believes it's a different network) → routes via gateway → gateway drops/routes locally. Fix: push /23 mask via DHCP option 1. All hosts renew DHCP → get /23 mask → can now ARP across both halves. Cutover risk: during transition hosts with /24 and /23 mixed on same segment → communication failures between them.
aspect_key: subnet mask and prefix split determine network id vs host space

question: A frame arrives at a switch with a destination MAC of FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF. A frame arrives with a destination MAC of 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E. A frame arrives with a destination of 01:00:5E:00:00:01. Describe the switching behavior for each frame and explain how each relates to the encapsulation of network-layer traffic.
choice_a: All three frames are flooded out all ports because the switch treats all non-unicast addresses identically
choice_b: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF is a Layer 2 broadcast — the switch floods the frame out all ports except the incoming port; this carries Layer 3 broadcasts (ARP; DHCP Discover). 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E is a unicast MAC — the switch looks it up in the MAC address table; if found it forwards out the specific port; if unknown it floods (unknown unicast flooding); this is standard encapsulated unicast IP traffic. 01:00:5E:00:00:01 has the first byte 01 (odd number) — the LSB of the first byte = 1 indicates multicast MAC; this specific address is the IPv4 multicast group 224.0.0.1 (all-hosts multicast); the switch floods it unless IGMP snooping is configured to selectively forward
choice_c: The switch uses TTL values to determine whether to flood or forward each frame
choice_d: Multicast frames are dropped by switches because they cannot be entered into the unicast MAC address table
explanation: MAC address types: bit 40 (LSB of first byte): 0 = unicast; 1 = multicast/broadcast. FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF: all bits 1 → broadcast → flood. 00:1A... first byte 00 → LSB=0 → unicast → MAC table lookup. 01:00:5E...: first byte 01 → LSB=1 → multicast → flood (or IGMP-snooped forward). IPv4 multicast → MAC: 01:00:5E:xx:xx:xx where lower 23 bits map to the group IP. IGMP snooping: switch tracks IGMP Join messages; forwards multicast only to interested ports.
aspect_key: application data wrapped through transport; network; and data-link layers

question: An engineer must allocate IP space for a WAN point-to-point link connecting two routers. Why is a /30 the traditional choice; what does a /31 provide as an alternative; and calculate the usable addresses for each?
choice_a: /30 provides 4 usable hosts — ideal for two routers plus two backup hosts
choice_b: /30 has 2 host bits: 4 addresses total; 4-2=2 usable hosts (exactly one per router interface) plus 2 wasted addresses (network + broadcast); the /31 provides 2 addresses total with no network or broadcast address — RFC 3021 defined this as valid for point-to-point links since there is no need for broadcast on a two-device link; /31 wastes zero addresses and is supported on most modern network vendors; the traditional choice is /30 because older software required the 2-address overhead for compatibility; modern designs prefer /31 for address conservation on large WAN meshes with many point-to-point links
choice_c: A /31 cannot be used because it has no broadcast address and ARP would fail
choice_d: /30 is required by RFCs for all WAN links — /31 and /32 are invalid for router interfaces
explanation: Address comparison: /30: 4 addresses (network; host1; host2; broadcast). 2 usable. 2 wasted. /31 (RFC 3021): 2 addresses (host1; host2). 0 wasted. No broadcast; no network address. ARP on /31: not needed (point-to-point; only 2 devices; no ambiguity). Large WAN: 1000 point-to-point links. /30: 2000 addresses wasted. /31: 0 wasted → saves 2000 addresses. Cisco: supports /31 on interfaces. Use case: provider backbone; MPLS core links.
aspect_key: 2^n - 2 model for assignable hosts in subnet

question: If a DNS CNAME record points to a hostname whose A record later changes, what happens to name resolution through the CNAME, and why is that design useful?
choice_a: www stops working because CNAME records cannot point to addresses that have changed
choice_b: www.corp.example.com continues working — the CNAME points to internal-server.corp.example.com; when resolved the CNAME directs the resolver to look up the A record for internal-server.corp.example.com; finding 10.0.0.75 (the updated record) the resolver returns this to the client; only one A record update was needed to fix both internal-server.corp.example.com and www.corp.example.com; if a separate A record had been created for www it would still point to 10.0.0.50 and would need to be manually updated separately — creating a maintenance risk where one record is updated and the other is forgotten
choice_c: A CNAME record caches the IP independently of the A record so it would still return 10.0.0.50
choice_d: CNAME records are only valid for the same domain — www.corp.example.com cannot CNAME to internal-server.corp.example.com because they are in the same zone
explanation: CNAME chain: www CNAME → internal-server. A → 10.0.0.75. Resolver follows chain: www.corp.example.com? → CNAME: follow internal-server.corp.example.com. internal-server.corp.example.com? → A: 10.0.0.75. Return 10.0.0.75. Maintenance advantage: one canonical A record; many CNAMEs pointing to it. IP changes: update 1 A record → all CNAMEs automatically updated. Separate A records: update each individually → error-prone. Real-world: mail.example.com; ftp.example.com; files.example.com all CNAMEs → server.example.com A record.
aspect_key: records; zones; resolver behavior; and lookup workflows

question: A classful Class A network 10.0.0.0/8 is subnetted using the mask 255.255.0.0. How many subnets does this create; what is the usable host count per subnet; and if each subnet must support a different physical building which operational challenge arises when the building count grows beyond the available subnets?
choice_a: 255.255.0.0 applied to a Class A creates 256 subnets of 65534 hosts and adding more buildings is impossible
choice_b: 255.255.0.0 = /16. Applied to 10.0.0.0/8: borrowed subnet bits = 16-8 = 8 bits. Subnets = 2^8 = 256. Host bits = 16. Usable hosts = 2^16 - 2 = 65534. When building count grows beyond 256: the /8 space allows sub-subnetting (VLSM) — each /16 subnet can be divided further; or the organization must use a smaller subnet mask creating more subnets (e.g. /20 → 2^12 = 4096 subnets); the operational challenge is the initial subnet design was fixed at /16 — adding buildings beyond 256 requires renumbering existing subnets or implementing VLSM within the existing /16 blocks; RFC 1918 private 10.0.0.0/8 provides ample space for redesign
choice_c: Class A networks cannot be subnetted with a /16 mask because the mask must match the classful boundary
choice_d: 255.255.0.0 creates 65534 subnets because the host bits become the subnet counter
explanation: 10.0.0.0/8 → /16: 8 borrowed bits (second octet) → 2^8=256 subnets. Third and fourth octets = host bits. 2^16-2=65534 hosts. Operational challenge: 256 is a soft limit only at /16 mask. Solution: sub-subnet. 10.1.0.0/16 can be divided into /24 subnets: 256 additional subnets within. VLSM within 10.0.0.0/8 allows millions of subnets at various sizes. True limit: 10.0.0.0/8 has 2^24 = 16.7M addresses; maximum /30 subnets = 4M.
aspect_key: a/b/c defaults as historical foundation for subnet logic

question: Two hosts on the same /24 subnet are communicating. Host A sends 1000 TCP segments each containing 1460 bytes of application data. Trace the complete headers that wrap the first segment at each layer of the encapsulation chain and calculate the total overhead bytes per segment.
choice_a: There is no overhead because encapsulation only adds labels that don't occupy bandwidth
choice_b: First segment contents: 1460 bytes application data + 20-byte TCP header = 1480-byte IP payload. IP header: 20 bytes (src IP; dst IP; TTL; Protocol=6; etc.) → IP packet = 1500 bytes. Ethernet frame header: 14 bytes (6 dst MAC + 6 src MAC + 2 type/length field) + 4-byte FCS trailer = 18 bytes overhead. Total frame = 1500 + 18 = 1518 bytes. Overhead: TCP (20) + IP (20) + Ethernet (18) = 58 bytes per segment. Efficiency = 1460/1518 = 96.2%. Over 1000 segments: 58000 bytes of overhead vs 1460000 bytes of data
choice_c: TCP/IP encapsulation adds only 2 bytes per segment because the headers are compressed in modern Ethernet
choice_d: Ethernet frames do not include headers — the MAC address is part of the IP packet
explanation: Encapsulation stack per segment: Application: 1460 bytes. TCP header: 20 bytes (src port; dst port; seq num; ACK; flags; window; checksum; urgent). IP header: 20 bytes (version; IHL; DSCP; total length; ID; flags; fragment offset; TTL; protocol; header checksum; src IP; dst IP). Ethernet: 14-byte header + 4-byte FCS = 18 bytes. Grand total: 1518 bytes per frame. Optional overhead: VLAN tag (802.1Q) adds 4 bytes = 1522 bytes maximum.
aspect_key: application data wrapped through transport; network; and data-link layers

question: A developer is troubleshooting why a large file upload over TCP to a remote server is very slow even though a speed test shows 100 Mbps available. The round-trip time to the server is 200ms. Calculate the maximum theoretical TCP throughput based on window size 65535 bytes and explain why the developer must use window scaling to achieve better performance.
choice_a: Large window sizes always cause slow connections because they increase CPU overhead on the router
choice_b: Maximum throughput with 65535-byte window and 200ms RTT: throughput = window_size / RTT = 65535 bytes / 0.200 seconds = 327675 bytes/s = 2.6 Mbps — far below the 100 Mbps available; the TCP receive window (advertised in 16-bit field; max 65535 bytes = 64 KB without scaling) was designed for the lower-latency networks of the 1980s; window scaling (RFC 7323) extends the window to up to 1 GB using a shift multiplier negotiated in the SYN; with a 1 GB window and 200ms RTT: 1000 MB / 0.2s = 5 Gbps theoretical maximum — far exceeding the link speed
choice_c: TCP automatically scales the window based on bandwidth so the developer's app does not need changes
choice_d: The 200ms RTT is irrelevant to TCP throughput — only the bandwidth limit matters
explanation: Bandwidth-delay product: BDP = bandwidth × RTT = 100 Mbps × 0.2s = 20 Mb = 2.5 MB. TCP window must be ≥ BDP to keep the pipe full. 65535 bytes = 64 KB << 2.5 MB → pipe is mostly empty. Window scaling: SYN option "Wscale=6" → multiply received window × 2^6 = ×64. 65535 × 64 = 4 MB. BDP satisfied → full 100 Mbps utilized. Tools: iperf3; ss --info show TCP window size during transfers.
aspect_key: reliable sequencing; ack-driven retransmission; and handshake control

question: An organization needs to route traffic between two RFC 1918 private address spaces: its own 10.0.0.0/8 and an acquired company also using 10.0.0.0/8. Explain why this creates a routing conflict and describe the two technical solutions used in practice.
choice_a: Routing between two identical private ranges works automatically because NAT is applied at both networks
choice_b: Both networks use 10.0.0.0/8 — when a host in the acquiring company (10.1.0.0/24) tries to reach a host at the acquired company (also 10.1.0.0/24); the router cannot distinguish which 10.1.x.x destination is in which network — all 10.0.0.0/8 traffic goes to the local routing table; Solution 1: NAT overload (double-NAT) — translate one company's 10.x.x.x to a unique IP range (e.g. 172.16.x.x) before routing between them; the router applies NAT to make each company's addresses appear distinct. Solution 2: renumber one company's addressing to a different RFC 1918 range (e.g. change acquired company to 172.20.0.0/16); costly but eliminates ongoing NAT overhead; required for any direct routing
choice_c: The router uses TTL to distinguish packets from each network — lower TTL indicates the far network
choice_d: Identical RFC 1918 ranges can be routed between each other using different VLAN IDs for identification
explanation: Overlapping private space problem: both companies have 10.1.1.50. Router cannot route to "10.1.1.50" uniquely — ambiguous. NAT solution: site A router: translate incoming traffic from site B's 10.x.x.x → 172.20.x.x before forwarding. A's hosts see B's hosts as 172.20.x.x (unambiguous). VPN tunnel: traffic enters VPN; far-end NAT translates to non-conflicting range. Renumbering: clean solution; change one site to 172.16.0.0/12 or 192.168.0.0/16; requires reconfiguring every host; DHCP scope; firewall rule; and DNS record.
aspect_key: routable internet space vs rfc1918 internal ranges
